title: buildah should set hostname in /etc/hostname
body:
<!--
If you are reporting a new issue, make sure that we do not have any duplicates
already open. You can ensure this by searching the issue list for this
repository. If there is a duplicate, please close your issue and add a comment
to the existing issue instead.

If you suspect your issue is a bug, please edit your issue description to
include the BUG REPORT INFORMATION shown below. If you fail to provide this
information within 7 days, we cannot debug your issue and will close it. We
will, however, reopen it if you later provide the information.

---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST
-->

**Description**

Buildah does not set the hostname in /etc/hostname.
While working on the /etc/hosts changes I noticed a difference between containers created by podman and buildah. Buildah does not manage the /etc/hostname file.

See the code for podman here: https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/53b984f20fef3a66269029f10c6cd16d7b80fd4e/libpod/container_internal_linux.go#L1988-L1996 

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**
1. 
```
$ cat Containerfile 
FROM alpine
RUN hostname && cat /etc/hostname
```
2. `buildah build .`


**Describe the results you received:**
```
STEP 3/3: RUN hostname && cat /etc/hostname
96e5f672af17
localhost
```

**Describe the results you expected:**
```
STEP 3/3: RUN hostname && cat /etc/hostname
96e5f672af17
96e5f672af17
```


**Output of `buildah version`:**
latest main branch

comments.body:
@Luap99 Do we have any prior issue associated to this, although legacy docker honors behavior as suggested in this issue but newer buildkit keeps `hostname` as localhost by default. 

I tried this with buildkit and here is the output it keeps `/etc/hostname` as `localhost`. We could do this change but it breaks parity with buildkit.

```console
#1 [internal] load .dockerignore
#1 transferring context: 2B done
#1 ...
 
#2 [internal] load build definition from Dockerfile
#2 transferring dockerfile: 84B done
#2 DONE 0.8s
 
#1 [internal] load .dockerignore
#1 DONE 1.0s
 
#3 [internal] load metadata for docker.io/library/alpine:latest
#3 DONE 3.2s
 
#4 [1/3] FROM docker.io/library/alpine@sha256:4edbd2beb5f78b1014028f4fbb99f3237d9561100b6881aabbf5acce2c4f9454
#4 resolve docker.io/library/alpine@sha256:4edbd2beb5f78b1014028f4fbb99f3237d9561100b6881aabbf5acce2c4f9454
#4 resolve docker.io/library/alpine@sha256:4edbd2beb5f78b1014028f4fbb99f3237d9561100b6881aabbf5acce2c4f9454 0.3s done
#4 DONE 0.3s
 
#4 [1/3] FROM docker.io/library/alpine@sha256:4edbd2beb5f78b1014028f4fbb99f3237d9561100b6881aabbf5acce2c4f9454
#4 DONE 0.7s
 
#4 [1/3] FROM docker.io/library/alpine@sha256:4edbd2beb5f78b1014028f4fbb99f3237d9561100b6881aabbf5acce2c4f9454
#4 sha256:df9b9388f04ad6279a7410b85cedfdcb2208c0a003da7ab5613af71079148139 0B / 2.81MB 0.2s
#4 sha256:df9b9388f04ad6279a7410b85cedfdcb2208c0a003da7ab5613af71079148139 2.81MB / 2.81MB 0.8s
#4 sha256:df9b9388f04ad6279a7410b85cedfdcb2208c0a003da7ab5613af71079148139 2.81MB / 2.81MB 0.8s done
#4 DONE 1.9s
 
#4 [1/3] FROM docker.io/library/alpine@sha256:4edbd2beb5f78b1014028f4fbb99f3237d9561100b6881aabbf5acce2c4f9454
#4 extracting sha256:df9b9388f04ad6279a7410b85cedfdcb2208c0a003da7ab5613af71079148139
#4 extracting sha256:df9b9388f04ad6279a7410b85cedfdcb2208c0a003da7ab5613af71079148139 0.4s done
#4 DONE 2.3s
 
#5 [2/3] RUN hostname
#5 0.402 buildkitsandbox
#5 DONE 1.5s
 
#6 [3/3] RUN cat /etc/hostname
#6 0.679 localhost
#6 DONE 1.0s
```

If podman sets hostname in /etc/hostname then buildah should too. These small differences between podman and buildah containers are just confusing for users.

Well than this is a bug in builkit too. There is no reason why /etc/hostname should not match the actual hostname from the uts namespace.

@Luap99 Yes I think this might be an issue with buildkit. Let me check if this can be fixed in buildkit as well so we can be sure that we are in parity and it does not break any downstream implementation because of parity diff.
I don't think we should hold up fixing this for buildkit to fix it.  We don't claim to be 100% compatible with buildkit, so this is not an issue.
